topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed with an irritating, relentless buzz. Sarah stared at the screen, her breath catching in her throat as the numbers loaded. It was a performance review summary, and the disparity between her salary and that of Mark, the intern who barely seemed to contribute anything, was stark. Her fingers, resting on the mouse, began to tremble. She reread the figures, her eyes darting back and forth, searching for a mistake, a typo, anything to justify the impossible reality unfolding before her. A cold knot tightened in her stomach, squeezing her chest until drawing a full breath felt like a conscious effort. When Mark finally walked past her cubicle, whistling a tuneless melody, Sarah instinctively hunched her shoulders, pulling her cardigan tighter around herself, wishing the floor would simply swallow her whole.

[story 2]

"Just... let me see that again, please?" Liam asked, his voice a strained whisper. He held up the spreadsheet, his knuckles white where he gripped the edge of the desk. The data confirmed what Sarah had suspected: the pay scale for the junior analyst position was wildly different. A wave of nausea washed over her, making the room seem to tilt slightly. She felt a sudden, intense pressure behind her eyes, and she squeezed them shut for a moment, trying to physically stop the influx of unwelcome information. When Liam finally looked up, his expression was one of utter defeat, and Sarah could only manage a small, jerky nod, her gaze fixed firmly on the beige carpet, unable to meet his eyes or process the sheer imbalance of the situation.

[story 3]

It happened during a routine system audit. Alex was cross-referencing departmental budgets when the discrepancy leaped out at him. The figures showed that Chloe, a relatively new team member, was earning nearly double what he was making. Alex’s hand started shaking so badly he had to consciously stop it mid-air. He felt a distinct chill run down his spine, despite the warmth of the office. He leaned back in his chair, the cheap plastic groaning under his weight, and stared blankly at the wall opposite him. Every sound in the open-plan office seemed amplified—the keyboard clicks, the distant phone ringing—all grating against the sudden, profound sense of wrongness settling deep in his core. He found himself gripping his pen so tightly the plastic threatened to crack.
